name: Actively Open-Minded Thinking about Evidence Scale
description: The Actively Open-Minded Thinking about Evidence (AOT-E) scale measures the meta-belief that one's beliefs should be revised in response to evidence. It was derived from the Stanovich and West (2007) AOT scale by Pennycook et al. (2020), who selected 8 items related to belief mutability from four AOT subscales (AOT, belief identification, dogmatism, and openness values). Participants respond on a 6-point scale from 1 (Strongly disagree) to 6 (Strongly agree). Items 3, 4, 5, 7, and 8 are reverse-scored. Higher total scores indicate more actively open-minded thinking about evidence. The scale was validated in a Brazilian sample and published in Judgment and Decision Making (Bonafé-Pontes et al., 2025).
citation: Pennycook, G., Cheyne, J. A., Koehler, D. J., & Fugelsang, J. A. (2020). On the belief that beliefs should change according to evidence: Implications for conspiratorial, moral, paranormal, political, religious, and science beliefs. Judgment and Decision Making, 15(4), 476–498. Brazilian validation: Bonafé-Pontes, A., Costa Bastos, R., & Pilati, R. (2025). Actively Open-Minded Thinking About Evidence (AOT-E) Scale: Adaptation and evidence of validity in a Brazilian sample. Judgment and Decision Making, 20, e3.

name: Adapted Historical Loss Scale
description: 7-item measure adapted for Native Hawaiian populations assessing frequency of thinking about historical losses. 6-point Likert scale (1=Never to 6=Several times a day). Two subscales: Loss of Culture (items 1-3) and Intergenerational Loss (items 4-5), plus Distrust and Destruction of Traditional Foods (items 6-7).
citation: Antonio, M. C. K., Keaulana, S., Ing, C. T., Williams, M., Dillard, A., Kekauoha, B. P., Kenney, A., Farahi, A., & Kaholokula, J. K. (2023). Frontiers in Public Health. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t96532-000]

dimensions.name: Distrust and Destruction of Traditional Foods

name: AI Attitude Scale
description: 4-item measure of general attitude toward artificial intelligence, covering perceived usefulness, adoption intention, and societal impact. Scores range from 1 to 10.
citation: Grassini, S. (2023). Development and validation of the AI attitude scale (AIAS-4): A brief measure of general attitude toward artificial intelligence. Frontiers in Psychology, 14, 1191628. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1191628

name: Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test
description: 10-item WHO screening tool for hazardous and harmful alcohol use. Scores range from 0 to 40.
citation: Saunders, J. B., Aasland, O. G., Babor, T. F., de la Fuente, J. R., & Grant, M. (1993). Development of the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT). Addiction, 88(6), 791-804. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1360-0443.1993.tb02093.x

name: Alcohol Use History
description: 14-item alcohol use history questionnaire. Collects demographics of drinking history including age of onset, quit attempts, treatment history, and consumption patterns. No scored dimensions — informational/clinical use.
license_explanation: Public domain. In-house measure developed at UW-Madison ARC.

name: Belief in Science Scale
description: 10-item scale measuring how important science is to individuals and how effective they perceive its methods to be in describing the world and uncovering truth. 6-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly disagree, 6 = Strongly agree). Single factor, alpha = .86.
citation: Farias, M., Newheiser, A.-K., Kahane, G., & de Toledo, Z. (2013). Scientific faith: Belief in science increases in the face of stress and existential anxiety. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 49(6), 1210–1213. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2013.05.008
license_explanation: Published under Creative Commons Attribution License.

name: Berlin Numeracy Test
description: 4-item adaptive test of statistical numeracy and risk literacy. Adaptive mode presents 2-3 questions based on correctness; non-adaptive mode presents all 4. Supports numeric free-entry or multiple-choice format.
citation: Cokely, E. T., Galesic, M., Schulz, E., Ghazal, S., & Garcia-Retamero, R. (2012). Measuring risk literacy: The Berlin Numeracy Test. Judgment and Decision Making, 7(1), 25-47.
license_explanation: Published in Judgment and Decision Making, which publishes all articles under Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license.

name: Brief COPE Inventory
description: 28-item self-report measure of coping strategies. 14 subscales with 2 items each: Self-Distraction, Active Coping, Denial, Substance Use, Emotional Support, Instrumental Support, Behavioral Disengagement, Venting, Positive Reframing, Planning, Humor, Acceptance, Religion, and Self-Blame. 4-point scale (0–3). No total score; subscales are interpreted individually.
citation: Carver, C. S. (1997). You want to measure coping but your protocol's too long: Consider the Brief COPE. International Journal of Behavioral Medicine, 4(1), 92–100. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327558ijbm0401_6
license_explanation: You are welcome to use all scales of the Brief COPE, or to choose selected scales for use. Feel free as well to adapt the language for whatever time scale you are interested in. (Charles S. Carver, http://www.psy.miami.edu/faculty/ccarver/sclCOPEF.html)

name: Brief Emotion Dysregulation Scale
description: 12-item Brief Emotion Dysregulation Scale with 3 subscales: Sensitivity (3 items), Lability (3 items), and Consequences (6 items). 4-point Likert (1=Strongly disagree to 4=Strongly agree). Items 6, 11, 12 are reverse-scored. Higher scores indicate greater emotion dysregulation.
citation: Wycoff, A. M., Griffin, S. A., Helle, A. C., Haney, A. M., Watts, A. L., & Trull, T. J. (2023). The Brief Emotion Dysregulation Scale: Development, preliminary validation, and recommendations for use. Assessment, 31(2), 335-349. https://doi.org/10.1177/10731911231161800
license_explanation: Authors have made the scale freely available for research use.

name: Brief Fear of Negative Evaluation Scale - Straightforward
description: 8-item scale consisting of the straightforward (non-reverse-worded) items from the Brief Fear of Negative Evaluation Scale. Measures fear and worry about receiving negative evaluation from others. Has superior psychometric properties compared to the full 12-item BFNE.
citation: Leary, M. R. (1983). A brief version of the Fear of Negative Evaluation Scale. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 9(3), 371-375. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167283093007
license_explanation: All 12 BFNE items (including the 8 straightforward items) are reproduced in Table 4 of Tavoli et al. (2009), BMC Psychiatry, 9, 42, published under CC BY 2.0. PMC2714515.

name: Brief Resilient Coping Scale
description: 4-item measure of resilient coping tendencies. Scores range from 4 to 20. Low resilience: 4-13, medium: 14-16, high: 17-20.
citation: Sinclair, V. G., & Wallston, K. A. (2004). The development and psychometric evaluation of the Brief Resilient Coping Scale. Assessment, 11(1), 94-101. https://doi.org/10.1177/1073191103258144

name: Bullshit Receptivity Scale
description: The Bullshit Receptivity Scale (BSR) assesses the tendency to perceive pseudo-profound but meaningless statements as profound. Participants rate 30 statements on a 1–5 profundity scale. The 30 statements include 10 pseudo-profound bullshit items (randomly generated from buzzword generators), 10 motivational quotations (conventionally profound), and 10 mundane statements (clearly not profound). The primary measure is the mean rating of the 10 pseudo-profound items; higher scores indicate greater receptivity to bullshit. Secondary measures assess sensitivity (bullshit minus motivational rating difference) and response bias.
citation: Pennycook, G., Cheyne, J. A., Barr, N., Koehler, D. J., & Fugelsang, J. A. (2015). On the reception and detection of pseudo-profound bullshit. Judgment and Decision Making, 10(6), 549–563.

name: Burnout Assessment Tool
description: 33-item Burnout Assessment Tool: 23 core items across four subscales (Exhaustion, Mental Distance, Cognitive Impairment, Emotional Impairment) plus 10 secondary symptom items (Psychological Distress, Psychosomatic Complaints). 5-point frequency scale (1=Never to 5=Always).
citation: Schaufeli, W. B., Desart, S., & De Witte, H. (2020). Burnout Assessment Tool (BAT)—Development, validity, and reliability. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 17(24), 9495. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph17249495

name: Burnout Assessment Tool – Short Form
description: 12-item short form of the Burnout Assessment Tool measuring core burnout symptoms across four subscales: Exhaustion (4 items), Mental Distance (3 items), Emotional Impairment (3 items), and Cognitive Impairment (2 items). Items use a 5-point frequency scale (1=Never to 5=Always). Subscale scores are means (range 1–5); a core burnout score is computed as the mean of Exhaustion and Mental Distance subscales. Higher scores indicate greater burnout.
citation: Schaufeli, W. B., De Witte, H., & Desart, S. (2020). Manual Burnout Assessment Tool (BAT) – Version 2.0. KU Leuven, Belgium. https://burnoutassessmenttool.be
license_explanation: CC BY 4.0. Published in IJERPH (MDPI open access).

name: Burnout Assessment Tool – Student Version
description: 33-item Burnout Assessment Tool — Student Version: 23 core items adapted for students across four subscales (Exhaustion, Mental Distance, Cognitive Impairment, Emotional Impairment) plus 10 secondary symptom items (Psychological Distress, Psychosomatic Complaints). 5-point frequency scale (1=Never to 5=Always). Short 12-item version (BAT-S-12) uses items marked with asterisk: 1,3,4,9,11,13,14,17,18,19,20,23.
citation: Schaufeli, W. B., De Witte, H., & Desart, S. (2020). Burnout Assessment Tool – Student Version (BAT-S). https://burnoutassessmenttool.be/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/BAT-S_ENG.pdf

name: Burnout Assessment Tool – Ultra-Short Form
description: 4-item ultra-short version of the Burnout Assessment Tool (one item per core subscale: Exhaustion, Mental Distance, Cognitive Impairment, Emotional Impairment). Items use a 5-point frequency scale (1=Never to 5=Always). Total score is the mean of all 4 items (range 1–5). Intended for group/population-level screening, not individual diagnosis.
citation: Hadžibajramović, E., Schaufeli, W. B., & De Witte, H. (2024). The ultra-short version of the Burnout Assessment Tool (BAT-4)—development, validation, and measurement invariance across countries, age and gender. PLOS ONE, 19(2), e0297843. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0297843
license_explanation: CC BY 4.0. Published in IJERPH (MDPI open access).

name: CAGE Questionnaire
description: 4-item screening tool for alcohol problems. Acronym: Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener. Yes/No responses scored 0–1. Total score 0–4; a score of 2 or more suggests problem drinking.
citation: Ewing, J. A. (1984). Detecting alcoholism: The CAGE questionnaire. JAMA, 252(14), 1905–1907. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1984.03350140051025
license_explanation: Believed to be in the public domain. Ewing (1984), the original author, encourages widespread use. See also: https://camcops.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tasks/cage.html

name: Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire
description: 25-item self-report measure of camouflaging behaviors used by autistic and non-autistic adults. Assesses three factors: compensation (strategies to compensate for social difficulties), masking (presenting a non-autistic persona), and assimilation (strategies to fit into social situations).
citation: Hull, L., Mandy, W., Lai, M. C., Baron-Cohen, S., Allison, C., Smith, P., & Petrides, K. V. (2019). Development and validation of the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire (CAT-Q). Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders, 49(3), 819-833. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-018-3792-6
license_explanation: Published in Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders as Open Access under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Cardiac Distress Inventory Short Form
description: 12-item measure of distress in cardiac patients. Each item first asks whether the issue has been experienced (Yes/No), then if Yes, rates distress from 0 (no distress) to 3 (severe distress). Total score ranges from 0 to 36; scores >13 indicate significant distress warranting completion of the full 55-item CDI.
citation: Le Grande, M. R., Murphy, B. M., Rogerson, M. C., Ski, C. F., Amerena, J., Smith, J. A., Hoover, V., Alvarenga, M. E., Higgins, R. O., Thompson, D. R., & Jackson, A. C. (2023). Development of a short form of the Cardiac Distress Inventory. BMC Cardiovascular Disorders, 23, 408. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12872-023-03439-w

name: Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale
description: 20-item self-report depression scale measuring depressive symptoms over the past week. Scores range from 0 to 60, with a threshold of 16 indicating clinically significant depression.
citation: Radloff, L. S. (1977). The CES-D scale: A self-report depression scale for research in the general population. Applied Psychological Measurement, 1(3), 385-401.
license_explanation: The CES-D is in the public domain and free to use without permission. See https://cesd-r.com/

name: Clinical Impairment Assessment 3.0
description: 16-item measure of psychosocial impairment due to eating disorder features over the past 28 days. Three subscales: Personal Impairment (6 items, range 0–18), Social Impairment (5 items, range 0–15), and Cognitive Impairment (5 items, range 0–15). Global score ranges from 0 to 48; a score of 16 or above may indicate clinical significance.
citation: Bohn, K., Doll, H. A., Cooper, Z., O'Connor, M., Palmer, R. L., & Fairburn, C. G. (2008). The measurement of impairment due to eating disorder psychopathology. Behaviour Research and Therapy, 46(10), 1105-1110. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.brat.2008.06.012

name: Clinical Outcomes in Routine Evaluation 10 (CORE-10)
description: 10-item scale. Auto-converted from CamCOPS; requires manual review.
citation: Barkham et al. (2001), PMID `11393596 <https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11393596>`_
license_explanation: CamCOPS IP notes: - "Any organisation is free to reproduce CORE instruments in software, as well as on paper, under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NoDerivatives 4.0 International (CC BY-ND 4.0) licence without payment of any licence fee (see http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nd/4.0/ for a human re

name: Cognitive Fusion Questionnaire
description: 7-item self-report measure of cognitive fusion — the degree to which thoughts dominate and regulate behavior. Items use a 7-point scale (1=Never to 7=Always). A single total score is computed as the sum of all 7 items (range 7–49); higher scores indicate greater cognitive fusion.
citation: Gillanders, D. T., Bolderston, H., Bond, F. W., Dempster, M., Flaxman, P. E., Campbell, L., Kerr, S., Tansey, L., Noel, P., Ferenbach, C., Masley, S., Roach, L., Lloyd, J., May, L., Clarke, S., & Remington, B. (2014). The development and initial validation of the Cognitive Fusion Questionnaire. Behavior Therapy, 45(1), 83–101. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.beth.2013.09.001
license_explanation: Free for research and clinical use (Association for Contextual Behavioral Science, ACBS).

name: Cognitive Load Theory Questionnaire
description: 15-item scale measuring three types of cognitive load during learning
citation: Krieglstein, F., Beege, M., Rey, G. D., Sanchez-Stockhammer, C., & Schneider, S. (2023). Development and validation of a theory-based questionnaire to measure different types of cognitive load. Educational Psychology Review, 35(1), 37. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10648-023-09738-0

name: Cognitive Reflection Test — Alternate Form
description: 4-item alternate form of the Cognitive Reflection Test, with 4 decoy questions. Measures the tendency to override incorrect intuitive responses and engage in further reflection. Score range 0–4.
citation: Thomson, K. S., & Oppenheimer, D. M. (2016). Investigating an alternate form of the cognitive reflection test. Judgment and Decision Making, 11(1), 99-113.

name: Cognitive Style Questionnaire — Short Form
description: 72-item measure (8 scenarios × 9 items) of negative cognitive style. 5 subscales: Internality, Globality, Stability, Negative Consequences, Self-Worth Implications. 5-point Likert (1=Strongly agree to 5=Strongly disagree). Higher scores = more negative cognitive style. Many items reverse-scored.
citation: Meins, E., McCarthy-Jones, S., Fernyhough, C., Lewis, G., Bentall, R. P., & Alloy, L. B. (2012). Assessing negative cognitive style: Development and validation of a short-form version of the Cognitive Style Questionnaire. Personality and Individual Differences, 52, 581-585.
license_explanation: Published under Creative Commons Attribution license. Meins, E., McCarthy-Jones, S., Fernyhough, C., Lewis, G., Bentall, R. P., & Alloy, L. B. (2012). Assessing negative cognitive style: Development and validation of a short-form version of the Cognitive Style Questionnaire. Personality and Individual Differences, 52, 581-585. doi:10.1016/j.paid.2011.11.026

name: Compulsive Exercise Test (CET)
description: A 24-item, 5-subscale self-report scale relating to compulsive exercise, applicable in the context of eating disorders. Each statement is rated from 0 (never true) to 5 (always true). History and guide Auto-converted from CamCOPS; requires manual review.
citation: Taranis L, Touyz S, Meyer C (2011). Disordered eating and exercise: development and preliminary validation of the compulsive exercise test (CET). *European Eating Disorders Review* 19: 256. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21584918/
license_explanation: CamCOPS IP notes: - Original (Taranis 2010): copyright © Lorin Taranis, 2010. Licensed under a Creative Commons CC BY-NC-ND 2.5 licence, as above. | - Regarding the "ND" aspects: Creative Commons explain that "merely changing the format never creates a derivative", and that "CC licenses grant permission to use the li

name: COPE Inventory
description: 60-item multidimensional coping inventory assessing 15 conceptually distinct coping strategies (4 items each). Response scale 1–4. No total score; subscales are interpreted individually. Can be administered in dispositional or situational format.
citation: Carver, C. S., Scheier, M. F., & Weintraub, J. K. (1989). Assessing coping strategies: A theoretically based approach. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 56(2), 267–283. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.56.2.267
license_explanation: You are welcome to use all scales of the COPE, or to choose selected scales for use. Feel free as well to adapt the language for whatever time scale you are interested in. Be sure to adapt the instructions for completion, as well as the items themselves. (Charles S. Carver)

name: Copenhagen Burnout Inventory
description: 19-item burnout measure with three subscales: Personal Burnout (6 items), Work-Related Burnout (7 items), and Client-Related Burnout (6 items). Two response formats: frequency (Always→Never) and degree (Very high degree→Very low degree), both scored 0–100 in 25-point increments. Item 13 is reverse-scored. Subscale scores are means (0–100). Public domain.
citation: Kristensen, T. S., Borritz, M., Villadsen, E., & Christensen, K. B. (2005). The Copenhagen Burnout Inventory: A new tool for the assessment of burnout. Work & Stress, 19(3), 192–207. https://doi.org/10.1080/02678370500297720
license_explanation: Developed at the Danish National Research Centre for the Working Environment (NFA) with public funds. Freely available.

name: Coping Mechanisms and Coping Inventory for Stressful Situations
description: 16-item coping inventory measuring four strategies university students use when facing stressful situations. Developed for and validated in a Ghanaian sample.
citation: Quansah, F., Ankomah, F., Hagan, J. E. Jr., Srem-Sai, M., Frimpong, J. B., Sambah, F., & Schack, T. (2022). Development and validation of an inventory for stressful situations in university students involving coping mechanisms: An interesting cultural mix in Ghana. Psych, 4(2), 173–186. https://doi.org/10.3390/psych4020015

name: Cravings for Rest and Volitional Energy Expenditure Scale
description: 13-item scale measuring cravings for rest and volitional energy expenditure. Uses an 11-point scale (0-10). Two subscales: Cravings for Rest (5 items) and Volitional Energy Expenditure (8 items). Assesses current state desire for physical activity versus rest.
citation: Stults-Kolehmainen, M. A., Blacutt, M., Fogelman, N., Gilson, T. A., Stanforth, P. R., Divin, A. L., Bartholomew, J. B., Filgueiras, A., Ciccolo, J. T., Chamberlin, J. M., & Sinha, R. (2021). Frontiers in Psychology. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2021.613267. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t86675-000]
license_explanation: Items published in Stults-Kolehmainen et al. (2021), Frontiers in Psychology, which publishes under CC BY 4.0.

name: Decision Styles Questionnaire
description: 43-item self-report questionnaire measuring individual differences in decision-making styles and decisional self-esteem. Developed through factor analysis retaining 9 factors: Avoidant (5 items), Dependent (6 items), Vigilant (6 items), Brooding (5 items), Intuitive (5 items), Anxious (5 items), Spontaneous (4 items), Confident (5 items), and Respected (2 items). Confident and Respected together form Decisional Self-Esteem. Items rated on a 5-point scale (1 = Never/Almost Never, 5 = Always/Almost Always). Higher scores on each subscale indicate a stronger tendency toward that style.
citation: Leykin, Y., & DeRubeis, R. J. (2010). Decision-making styles and depressive symptomatology: Development of the Decision Styles Questionnaire. Judgment and Decision Making, 5(7), 506-515. https://doi.org/10.1017/S1930297500001674

name: Depression Anxiety Stress Scales-21
description: 21-item short form measuring depression, anxiety, and stress over the past week. Each subscale has 7 items scored 0-3. Multiply subscale sums by 2 to compare with DASS-42 norms (0-42 range per subscale).
citation: Lovibond, S. H., & Lovibond, P. F. (1995). Manual for the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (2nd ed.). Psychology Foundation of Australia.

name: Depression Anxiety Stress Scales-42
description: 42-item full-length measure of depression, anxiety, and stress over the past week. Each subscale has 14 items scored 0-3 (range 0-42 per subscale).
citation: Lovibond, S. H., & Lovibond, P. F. (1995). Manual for the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales (2nd ed.). Psychology Foundation of Australia.

name: Disgust Avoidance Questionnaire
description: 17-item questionnaire assessing how people cope with disgust-eliciting situations. Uses a 7-point Likert scale (1-7). Three overlapping subscales scored as unweighted sums: Prevention (PREV), Behavioral (BEH), and Cognitive (COG).
citation: von Spreckelsen, P., Jonker, N. C., Vugteveen, J., Wessel, I., Glashouwer, K. A., & de Jong, P. J. (2022). PLOS ONE. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0271915. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t82804-000]
license_explanation: Items published in von Spreckelsen et al. (2022), PLOS ONE, which publishes under CC BY 4.0.

name: Distress Thermometer
description: Screening tool for psychosocial distress in cancer patients. Part 1: a single 0–10 distress rating (the Distress Thermometer proper). Part 2 (optional): 36 yes/no problem checklist across 5 domains (Practical, Family, Emotional, Spiritual/religious, Physical) — this Problem List was used alongside the DT in the original study but is not part of the DT itself. A distress score of 4+ indicates clinically significant distress requiring further assessment. Public domain (US National Cancer Institute).
citation: Roth, A. J., Kornblith, A. B., Batel-Copel, L., Peabody, E., Scher, H. I., & Holland, J. C. (1998). Rapid screening for psychologic distress in men with prostate carcinoma. Cancer, 82(10), 1904–1908. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9587123/ / Lynch, J., Goodhart, F., Saunders, Y., & O'Connor, S. J. (2011). Screening for psychological distress in patients with lung cancer: results of a clinical audit evaluating the use of the patient Distress Thermometer. Supportive Care in Cancer, 19, 193–202. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00520-009-0799-8
license_explanation: Believed to be in the public domain. Made freely available by the US National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN).

name: Domain-Specific Risk-Taking Scale (Revised)
description: 30-item scale with up to three response modes per item: risk-taking likelihood (RT), risk perception (RP), and expected benefits (EB). Assesses five content domains: ethical, financial, health/safety, recreational, and social. For each item, RT is rated first (7-point: Extremely Unlikely to Extremely Likely), then RP (7-point: Not at all Risky to Extremely Risky), then optionally EB (7-point: No benefits at all to Great benefits). The EB mode can be enabled via the show_expected_benefits parameter. Items are presented in triplets grouped into randomizable sections. Subscale scores are means of 6 items per domain within each response mode.
citation: Blais, A.-R., & Weber, E. U. (2006). A Domain-Specific Risk-Taking (DOSPERT) scale for adult populations. Judgment and Decision Making, 1(1), 33-47.

name: DOSPERT Medical Risk Subscale
description: 6-item medical risk domain extension for the DOSPERT scale. Adds medical scenarios to the five existing DOSPERT content domains. Administered with the same three response modes as DOSPERT: risk-taking likelihood (RT), risk perception (RP), and optionally expected benefits (EB). Designed to be used alongside the base DOSPERT scale. Subscale scores are means of the 6 medical items within each response mode.
citation: Butler, S., Rosman, A., Seleski, S., Garcia, M., Lee, S., Barnes, J., & Schwartz, A. (2012). A medical risk attitude subscale for DOSPERT. Judgment and Decision Making, 7(2), 189-195.

name: Emotion Beliefs Questionnaire
description: 16-item self-report measure of beliefs about the controllability and usefulness of negative and positive emotions. Four valence-specific subscales (Negative-Controllability, Positive-Controllability, Negative-Usefulness, Positive-Usefulness), two composite scores (General-Controllability, General-Usefulness), and a Total score. Higher scores indicate stronger beliefs that emotions are uncontrollable and useless.
citation: Becerra, R., Preece, D. A., & Gross, J. J. (2020). Assessing beliefs about emotions: Development and validation of the Emotion Beliefs Questionnaire. PLOS ONE, 15(4), e0231395. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0231395

name: Emotion Regulation Questionnaire
description: 10-item measure of habitual emotion regulation strategies: Cognitive Reappraisal (6 items) and Expressive Suppression (4 items). 7-point Likert scale (1=Strongly disagree to 7=Strongly agree). The 6-item short form (ERQ-S) uses a subset of 3 Reappraisal + 3 Suppression items. Set use_short_form=1 to administer the ERQ-S.
citation: Gross, J. J., & John, O. P. (2003). Individual differences in two emotion regulation processes: Implications for affect, relationships, and well-being. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 85(2), 348–362. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.85.2.348 | ERQ-S: Preece, D. A., Petrova, K., Mehta, A., & Gross, J. J. (2023). The Emotion Regulation Questionnaire—Short Form (ERQ-S). Journal of Affective Disorders, 340, 855–861. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jad.2023.08.076
license_explanation: All 10 ERQ items and the ERQ-S mapping are published in the CC BY 4.0 licensed ERQ-S paper (Preece et al., 2023, J Affect Disord). Original ERQ also freely available from Stanford Psychophysiology Lab.

name: Emotional Vulnerability Scale
description: 16-item self-report measure of emotional vulnerability in university students. Assesses vulnerability in relation to everyday situations across four factors: vulnerability toward criticism or denial, worsening relationships, interpersonal discord, and procrastination and emotional avoidance.
citation: Takahashi, F., Ishizu, K., Matsubara, K., Ohtsuki, T., & Shimoda, Y. (2022). The development and validation of an emotional vulnerability scale for university students. Frontiers in Psychology, 13, 941250. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2022.941250
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychology under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

dimensions.name: Vulnerability toward Procrastination and Emotional Avoidance

name: Employee Multidimensional Trust in Leaders Scale
description: 15-item measure of employee trust in leaders across three dimensions. 5-point Likert scale (1=Strongly agree to 5=Strongly disagree). Note: higher scores indicate more disagreement. Three subscales: Authentic Behaviours (7 items), Interpersonal Connection and Care (5 items), and Consistent Communication (3 items).
citation: Fischer, S., Walker, A., & Hyder, S. (2023). Frontiers in Psychology. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t95997-000]

name: Epilepsy Surgery Inventory-55
description: 55-item health-related quality of life measure developed specifically for patients being evaluated for or having undergone epilepsy surgery. Adapted from the RAND 36-Item Health Survey (SF-36) plus 19 epilepsy-specific items covering cognitive function, role limitations, and overall quality of life. Covers 11 health concepts scored 0–100 (higher = better health/quality of life). NOTE: Exact item text requires the official RAND instrument — see URL above. Item text provided here is based on Vickrey et al. (1992) and the published scale structure.

license_explanation: Public Domain (RAND Corporation).

name: Explanation Satisfaction Scale
description: Standard scale for evaluating user satisfaction in explanations provided by AI and XAI systems.
citation:
Hoffman, R. R., Mueller, S. T., Klein, G., & Litman, J. (2018). Metrics for explainable AI: Challenges and prospects. arXiv preprint arXiv:1812.04608.
Hoffman, R. R., Mueller, S. T., Klein, G., & Litman, J. (2023). Measures for explainable AI: Explanation goodness, user satisfaction, mental models, curiosity, trust, and human-AI performance. Frontiers in Computer Science, 5, 1096257.

name: Fast Alcohol Screening Test (FAST)
description: 4-item rapid alcohol screening test derived from the AUDIT. Items 1–3 use a frequency scale (0–4); item 4 uses a 3-option scale (0, 2, 4). Total score range 0–16; FAST-positive if total ≥3. Public domain (UK NHS/Public Health England).
citation: Hodgson, R., Alwyn, T., John, B., Thom, B., & Smith, A. (2002). The FAST Alcohol Screening Test. Alcohol and Alcoholism, 37(1), 61–66. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11825859/
license_explanation: Believed to be in the public domain. Made freely available with encouragement to copy by the UK NHS Health Development Agency, NICE, and Public Health England. See CamCOPS IP documentation: https://camcops.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tasks/fast.html

name: Fear of War Scale
description: 13-item self-report measure assessing fear of war through two dimensions: experiential fear (emotional/cognitive reactions, items 1-7) and physiological fear (somatic reactions, items 8-13). 5-point Likert scale. Higher scores indicate greater fear of war. Originally developed in Hungarian.
citation: Kalcza-Janosi, K., Kotta, I., Marschalko, E. E., & Szabo, K. (2023). The Fear of War Scale (FOWARS): Development and initial validation. Social Sciences, 12(5), 283. https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci12050283
license_explanation: Published in MDPI Social Sciences under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire
description: 39-item measure of five facets of mindfulness: Observing, Describing, Acting with Awareness, Non-Judging, and Non-Reactivity. 5-point scale (1=Never or very rarely true to 5=Very often or always true). 19 items are reverse-scored. Set use_short_form=1 to administer the 15-item FFMQ-15 subset.
citation: Baer, R. A., Smith, G. T., Hopkins, J., Krietemeyer, J., & Toney, L. (2006). Using self-report assessment methods to explore facets of mindfulness. Assessment, 13(1), 27–45. https://doi.org/10.1177/1073191105283504
license_explanation: Items published under CC BY 3.0 in Gu et al. (2016), Psychological Assessment, 28(7), 791–802. https://psycnet.apa.org/fulltext/2016-17965-001.html. Also freely available from developer at ruthbaer.com.

name: Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire – 15-Item Short Form
description: 15-item short form of the Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ), assessing five facets of mindfulness: Observing, Describing, Acting with Awareness, Non-Judging of inner experience, and Non-Reactivity to inner experience. Three items per facet. Derived from the FFMQ-39 (Baer et al., 2006) by selecting items with highest loadings for each facet. Each facet score is the mean of its three items (range 1–5); a total mindfulness score is the mean of all 15 items.
citation: Gu, J., Strauss, C., Crane, C., Barnhofer, T., Karl, A., Cavanagh, K., & Kuyken, W. (2016). Examining the factor structure of the 39-item and 15-item versions of the Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire before and after mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for people with recurrent depression. Psychological Assessment, 28(7), 791–802. https://doi.org/10.1037/pas0000263. Original FFMQ-39: Baer, R. A., Smith, G. T., Hopkins, J., Krietemeyer, J., & Toney, L. (2006). Using self-report assessment methods to explore facets of mindfulness. Assessment, 13(1), 27–45.
license_explanation: Items and scoring information distributed under CC BY 3.0 via ResearchGate.

name: Frost Multidimensional Perfectionism Scale — Brief
description: 8-item brief perfectionism measure with two subscales: Evaluative Concerns (4 items, derived from Concern over Mistakes/Doubts about Actions) and Strivings (4 items, derived from Personal Standards). 5-point Likert (1–5). Subscale scores are sums (range 4–20).
citation: Woodfin, V., Binder, P.-E., & Molde, H. (2020). A psychometric assessment of the Frost Multidimensional Perfectionism Scale-Brief. Frontiers in Psychology, 11, 1860. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.01860
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychology under CC BY 4.0.

name: Functional Meaning of Cash Rewards Scale
description: 7-item measure of the functional meaning employees attribute to cash rewards from their supervisor. Two subscales: Controlling Meaning (items 1-3) and Informative Meaning (items 4-7). 7-point Likert scale (1=Strongly Disagree to 7=Strongly Agree).
citation: Thibault Landry, A., Papachristopoulos, K., Gradito Dubord, M.-A., & Forest, J. (2022). Frontiers in Psychology. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t94383-000]

name: Gaming Disorder Symptom Questionnaire
description: 21-item Gaming Disorder Symptom Questionnaire with 3 subscales. Items 1-13 use a 5-point frequency scale (0=Never to 4=Almost daily). Items 14-21 use a two-part format: (a) frequency (0-4), then (b) yes/no continuation question (only if a≥1). Score for items 14-21 = a × b. Originally developed in Chinese.
citation: Zhang, L., Luo, T., Hao, W., Cao, Y., Yuan, M., & Liao, Y. (2022). Gaming Disorder Symptom Questionnaire: The development and validation of a screening tool for ICD-11 gaming disorder in adolescents. Frontiers in Psychiatry, 13, 848157. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyt.2022.848157
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychiatry under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Gaming Hazard Assessment Scale
description: 18-item risk screening tool for gaming disorder based on DSM-5 and ICD-11 criteria. Assesses three dimensions: out-of-control gaming, impairment of social functioning, and cognitive impairment. Total scores range from 0 to 54, with cutoffs for no risk (0-18), moderate risk (19-36), and high risk (37-54).
citation: Tang, Y., Liao, Z., Huang, S., Hao, J., Huang, Q., Huang, X., Chen, X., Lin, S., Li, Y., Qi, J., & Shen, H. (2022). Development and validation of a risk assessment tool for gaming disorder in China: The Gaming Hazard Assessment Scale. Frontiers in Public Health, 10, 870358. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2022.870358
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Public Health under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: General Decision-Making Style Scale
description: The General Decision-Making Style (GDMS) scale assesses five habitual decision-making styles: rational (systematic, logical approach), intuitive (reliance on feelings and hunches), dependent (seeking advice from others), avoidant (postponing or avoiding decisions), and spontaneous (quick, impulsive decisions). Participants rate 25 items on a 5-point agreement scale. Each subscale comprises 5 items; higher scores indicate greater use of that style. The GDMS is validated in Judgment and Decision Making in its Spanish adaptation (Salgado-Orellana et al., 2019).
citation: Scott, S. G., & Bruce, R. A. (1995). Decision-making style: The development and assessment of a new measure. Educational and Psychological Measurement, 55(5), 818–831. Spanish validation: Salgado-Orellana, N., Berrios-Martos, P., Guarino, L., & García-Martínez, J. M. A. (2019). Spanish validation of General Decision-Making Style scale: Sex invariance, sex differences and relationships with personality and coping styles. Judgment and Decision Making, 14(6), 670–683.

name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7
description: 7-item anxiety screening tool measuring symptom severity over the past 2 weeks. Scores range from 0 to 21.
citation: Spitzer, R. L., Kroenke, K., Williams, J. B., & Lowe, B. (2006). A brief measure for assessing generalized anxiety disorder: The GAD-7. Archives of Internal Medicine, 166(10), 1092-1097. https://doi.org/10.1001/archinte.166.10.1092
license_explanation: Pfizer released all PHQ and GAD-7 screeners without copyright restriction. The phqscreeners.com terms of use state: 'Content found at the PHQ Screeners site is expressly exempted from Pfizer's general copyright restrictions; content found on the PHQ Screeners site is free for download and use as stated within the PHQ Screeners site.' No permission is required to reproduce, translate, display, or distribute.

name: Generic Conspiracist Beliefs Scale
description: 15-item measure of generic conspiracist beliefs across 5 facets (3 items each). 5-point Likert scale (1=Definitely not true to 5=Definitely true). Subscale scores are the mean of their respective items.
citation: Brotherton, R., French, C. C., & Pickering, A. D. (2013). Measuring belief in conspiracy theories: The Generic Conspiracist Beliefs scale. Frontiers in Psychology, 4, 279. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00279. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t41554-000]

name: Geriatric Depression Scale (Short Form)
description: 15-item short form of the Geriatric Depression Scale. Uses a Yes/No response format. Designed for screening depression in older adults.
citation: Yesavage, J. A., Brink, T. L., Rose, T. L., Lum, O., Huang, V., Adey, M., & Leirer, V. O. (1982). Development and validation of a geriatric depression screening scale: A preliminary report. Journal of Psychiatric Research, 17(1), 37-49. https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-3956(82)90033-4 / Sheikh, J. I., & Yesavage, J. A. (1986). Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS): Recent evidence and development of a shorter version. Clinical Gerontologist, 5(1-2), 165-173. https://doi.org/10.1300/J018v05n01_09
license_explanation: The official GDS page at Stanford (maintained by Dr. Yesavage) states: 'The original scale is in the public domain due to it being partly the result of Federal support.' Development was supported by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs / VA Palo Alto Health Care System.

name: Impostor Phenomenon Short Scale
description: 3-item ultra-brief measure of impostor feelings. Unidimensional. 6-point scale (1=does not apply at all to 6=absolutely applies). Sum score range 3–18; higher scores indicate stronger impostor feelings.
citation: Jansen, M. P. (2024). Impostor phenomenon short scale (IPSS-3): A novel measure to capture impostor feelings in large-scale and longitudinal surveys. Frontiers in Psychology, 15, 1358279. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2024.1358279
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychology under CC BY 4.0.

name: Informed Consent
description: Generic IRB-style informed consent gate. Customise study_title, pi_name, and contact_email via parameters. Terminates and records a data row if the participant declines or is ineligible.

name: Integrative Vitality Scale
description: 22-item self-report measure of integrative vitality based on traditional Eastern medicine and modern psychology. Assesses two subscales: physical vitality (items 1-11) and psychological vitality (items 12-22). Item 11 is reverse-scored.
citation: Yoon, S. I., Park, H. Y., Chung, S. Y., & Kim, J. W. (2024). Development and validation of the Integrative Vitality Scale. Frontiers in Public Health, 12, 1452068. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2024.1452068
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Public Health under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: International Physical Activity Questionnaire Short Form
description: 7-item self-report measure of physical activity over the past 7 days. Yields raw day and minute counts for vigorous, moderate, and walking activity, plus sitting time. Official MET-minute scoring: Vigorous = 8.0 × days × min/day; Moderate = 4.0 × days × min/day; Walking = 3.3 × days × min/day. Categories: Inactive (<600 MET-min/week), Minimally Active (600–2999), HEPA Active (≥3000 and meets additional criteria). MET scores are calculated via computed variables.
citation: Craig, C. L., Marshall, A. L., Sjöström, M., Bauman, A. E., Booth, M. L., Ainsworth, B. E., Pratt, M., Ekelund, U., Yngve, A., Sallis, J. F., & Oja, P. (2003). International physical activity questionnaire: 12-country reliability and validity. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise, 35(8), 1381–1395. https://doi.org/10.1249/01.MSS.0000078924.61453.FB
license_explanation: The IPAQ questionnaire is publicly available, it is open access, and no permissions are required to use it.

citation: Levenson, H. (1981). Differentiating among internality, powerful others, and chance. In H. M. Lefcourt (Ed.), Research with the Locus of Control Construct (Vol. 1, pp. 15-63). Academic Press.

name: IPIP Need for Cognition Scale
description: IPIP representation of the Need for Cognition scale measuring tendency to engage in and enjoy effortful thinking.
citation: Cacioppo, J. T., & Petty, R. E. (1982). The need for cognition. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 42(1), 116-131.

name: IPIP Optimism Scale
description: IPIP items measuring dispositional optimism, designed to approximate the LOT-R.
citation: Scheier, M. F., Carver, C. S., & Bridges, M. W. (1994). Distinguishing optimism from neuroticism. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 67(6), 1063-1078.

name: IPIP Oregon Vocational Interest Scales
description: IPIP vocational interest scales measuring 8 broad interest domains.
citation: Pozzebon, J. A., Visser, B. A., Ashton, M. C., Lee, K., & Goldberg, L. R. (2010). Psychometric characteristics of a public-domain self-report measure of vocational interests. Journal of Personality Assessment, 92(2), 168-174.

name: IPIP Personality Disorder Traits (CAT-PD)
description: IPIP representation of the CAT-PD measuring 33 personality disorder-relevant trait dimensions.
citation: Simms, L. J., Goldberg, L. R., Roberts, J. E., Watson, D., Welte, J., & Rotterman, J. H. (2011). Computerized Adaptive Assessment of Personality Disorder. Psychological Assessment, 23(1), 111-126.

name: IPIP Self-Esteem Scale
description: IPIP items measuring self-esteem, designed to approximate the Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale.
citation: Rosenberg, M. (1965). Society and the Adolescent Self-Image. Princeton University Press.

name: IPIP Self-Monitoring Scale
description: IPIP items measuring self-monitoring tendencies.
citation: Snyder, M. (1974). Self-monitoring of expressive behavior. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 30(4), 526-537.

name: IPIP Social Desirability Scales
description: IPIP representation of the BIDR measuring impression management, self-deception, and cognitive failures.
citation: Paulhus, D. L. (1991). Measurement and control of response bias. In J. P. Robinson, P. R. Shaver, & L. S. Wrightsman (Eds.), Measures of Personality and Social Psychological Attitudes (pp. 17-59). Academic Press.

name: IPIP-HEXACO Personality Inventory
description: IPIP representation of the HEXACO-PI measuring 6 domains and 24 facets, including Honesty-Humility.
citation: Lee, K., & Ashton, M. C. (2004). Psychometric properties of the HEXACO personality inventory. Multivariate Behavioral Research, 39(2), 329-358.

name: IPIP-NEO Personality Inventory
description: IPIP representation of the NEO-PI-R measuring 5 domains and 30 facets of the Five Factor Model of personality.
citation: Goldberg, L. R. (1999). A broad-bandwidth, public-domain, personality inventory measuring the lower-level facets of several five-factor models. In I. Mervielde, I. Deary, F. De Fruyt, & F. Ostendorf (Eds.), Personality Psychology in Europe (Vol. 7, pp. 7-28). Tilburg University Press.

name: Kidney Disease Quality of Life 36-Item Short Form
description: 36-item health-related quality of life measure for patients with kidney disease, including those on dialysis. Contains a 12-item SF-12 generic core plus 3 kidney disease-specific subscales: Burden of Kidney Disease (4 items), Symptoms/Problems of Kidney Disease (12 items), and Effects of Kidney Disease on Daily Life (8 items). One additional overall health rating item (0–10 scale). All subscales scored 0–100 (higher = better quality of life). NOTE: SF-12 items are scored per the SF-12 manual (Ware et al., 1996); kidney-specific subscales are scored as means recoded to 0–100.
citation: Ware, J.E., Kosinki, M., Keller, S.D. (1996). SF-12: How to score the SF-12 Physical and Mental Health Summary Scales. Boston: The Health Institute; and Hays, R.D., Kallich, J.D., Mapes, D.L., Coons, S.J., & Carter, W.B. (1994). Development of the kidney disease quality of life (KDQOL) instrument. Quality of Life Research, 3(5), 329–338.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Life Crafting Scale
description: 9-item self-report measure of life crafting behaviors across cognitive meaning-making, social support seeking, and challenge seeking dimensions.
citation: Chen, S., van der Meij, L., van Zyl, L. E., & Demerouti, E. (2022). The Life Crafting Scale: Development and validation of a multi-dimensional meaning-making measure. Frontiers in Psychology, 13, 795686. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2022.795686

name: Maternal Antenatal Attachment Scale (MAAS)
description: 19-item measure of maternal attachment to the unborn baby during pregnancy. Each item has unique response options. CC BY 3.0 AU (Condon).
citation: Condon, J. T. (1993). The assessment of antenatal emotional attachment: Development of a questionnaire instrument. British Journal of Medical Psychology, 66(2), 167–183.
license_explanation: Copyright © John T Condon 2015. Open Access under Creative Commons Attribution License 3.0 AU. See CamCOPS IP documentation: https://camcops.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tasks/maas.html

name: Maximization Inventory
description: 34-item measure of maximizing tendency comprising three separate subscales: Alternative Search (12 items), Decision Difficulty (12 items), and Satisficing (10 items). The subscales should not be summed into a total score; they measure distinct and theoretically separable components of maximizing behavior.
citation: Turner, B. M., Rim, H. B., Betz, N. E., & Nygren, T. E. (2012). The Maximization Inventory. Judgment and Decision Making, 7(1), 48-60.

name: Mcq17
description: 17-item self-report measure.
citation: Heishman, S. J., Singleton, E. G., & Liguori, A. (2001). Marijuana Craving Questionnaire: Development and initial validation of a self-report instrument. Addiction, 96(7), 1023-1034.
license_explanation: Public domain: (C) US Government.

name: Mental Health Inventory (MHI-38)
description: 38-item measure of psychological distress and well-being developed for the Medical Outcomes Study. Most items use a 6-point frequency scale referring to the past month, but several items use alternate response formats (happiness, frequency, nervousness severity, depression severity, suicidal ideation). Yields 5 subscales: Anxiety (9 items), Depression (4 items), Loss of Behavioral/Emotional Control (10 items), General Positive Affect (10 items), and Emotional Ties (5 items). Two composite scores: Psychological Distress and Psychological Well-Being. Scores are item means transformed to 0–100: (mean − 1) / 5 × 100. Higher scores indicate better mental health.
citation: Veit, C.T., & Ware, J.E. (1983). The structure of psychological distress and well-being in general populations. Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, 51(5), 730-742. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-006X.51.5.730
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Mental Health Inventory-5
description: 5-item mental health screening measure derived from the RAND SF-36 (items 9b, 9c, 9d, 9f, 9h). Assesses psychological well-being and distress over the past 4 weeks. 6-point frequency scale (1=All of the time to 6=None of the time). Items 1, 2, 4 (anxiety/depression) are reverse-scored; items 3, 5 (positive affect) are forward-scored. Score range 5–30; lower scores indicate worse mental health. Widely used as a brief mental health screener.
citation: Berwick, D. M., Murphy, J. M., Goldman, P. A., Ware, J. E., Barsky, A. J., & Weinstein, M. C. (1991). Performance of a five-item mental health screening test. Medical Care, 29(2), 169–176. https://doi.org/10.1097/00005650-199102000-00008
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. MHI-5 items are a subset of the RAND SF-36.

name: Mental Health Test
description: 18-item complex mental health measure in a positive psychological framework. Five subscales: Well-being (W), Savoring (S), Creative and Executive Efficiency (C), Self-regulation (SR), and Resilience (R). 6-point Likert (1=Strongly disagree to 6=Strongly agree); item 18 uses a different format (1=Very bad to 6=Very good). SR items (2,8,16) and Resilience item 13 are reverse coded using 7-x transformation. Higher scores indicate better mental health.
citation: Zábó, V., Oláh, A., & Vargha, A. (2022). A new complex mental health test in a positive psychological framework. Frontiers in Psychology, 13. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2022.775622. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t95738-000]
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychology under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Michigan Alcohol Screening Test (MAST)
description: 24-item yes/no self-report screening test for alcohol use problems (Selzer, 1971). Items 1, 4, 6, 7 are reverse-scored (No = problem). Weighted scoring: most items score 2 points, items 3, 5, 9, 16 score 1 point, items 8, 19, 20 score 5 points. Total range 0–53. Norms: 0–3 no apparent problem, 4 suggests problem, 5+ indicates alcoholism. Public domain.
citation: Selzer, M. L. (1971). The Michigan Alcoholism Screening Test: The quest for a new diagnostic instrument. American Journal of Psychiatry, 127(12), 1653–1658. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/5565851/
license_explanation: Public domain. Described as 'a public-domain screening tool' by Michigan 24th District Court. See CamCOPS IP documentation: https://camcops.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tasks/mast.html

name: Modified Abbreviated Math Anxiety Scale
description: 9-item scale measuring math anxiety in children and adolescents. Uses a 5-point Likert scale (1=Low anxiety to 5=High anxiety). Single total score (sum of all items). Adapted from the AMAS for use with younger populations.
citation: Carey, E., Hill, F., Devine, A., & Szucs, D. (2017). The modified Abbreviated Math Anxiety Scale: A valid and reliable instrument for use with children and adolescents. Frontiers in Psychology. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2017.00011. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t66914-000]
license_explanation: Items published in Carey et al. (2017), Frontiers in Psychology, which publishes under CC BY 4.0.

name: Modified Maximizing Scale
description: 5-item measure of maximizing tendency that excludes the decision difficulty component present in earlier scales. Developed to reduce the confound between maximizing and depressive symptomatology. All items are content-free (not tied to specific consumer situations) and load on a single factor reflecting aspirations for high standards and extensive alternative search. The scale is typically treated as unidimensional, though items reflect two related facets: High Standards and Alternative Search.
citation: Lai, L. (2010). Maximizing without difficulty: A modified maximizing scale and its correlates. Judgment and Decision Making, 5(3), 164-175.

name: MOS 12-Item Short Form Health Survey
description: 12-item subset of the RAND 36-Item Health Survey (MOS-SFHF-36) covering physical functioning, role limitations (physical and emotional), bodily pain, general health, vitality, social functioning, and mental health. Items drawn from the public domain RAND-36. Mixed response formats. Higher scores indicate better health.
citation: Ware, J. E., Kosinski, M., & Keller, S. D. (1996). A 12-Item Short-Form Health Survey: Construction of scales and preliminary tests of reliability and validity. Medical Care, 34(3), 220–233. Items from: Hays, R. D., Sherbourne, C. D., & Mazel, R. M. (1993). The RAND 36-Item Health Survey 1.0. Health Economics, 2(3), 217–227.
license_explanation: Items are a subset of the RAND 36-Item Health Survey which is in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee. This is NOT the proprietary SF-12v2 from QualityMetric.

name: MOS 20-Item Short Form Health Survey
description: 20-item short form general health survey from the Medical Outcomes Study measuring six dimensions: Physical Functioning (6 items), Role Functioning (2 items), Social Functioning (1 item), Mental Health (5 items), Health Perceptions (5 items including general health), and Pain (1 item). Mixed response formats. Higher scores indicate better health. Public Domain (RAND Corporation).
citation: Stewart, A. L., Hays, R. D., & Ware, J. E. (1988). The MOS Short-Form General Health Survey: Reliability and validity in a patient population. Medical Care, 26(7), 724–735. https://doi.org/10.1097/00005650-198807000-00007
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain.

name: MOS 36-Item Short Form Health Survey (RAND-36)
description: 36-item health survey measuring 8 dimensions of health. Items use multiple response formats; standard RAND 0–100 subscale scoring requires post-hoc recoding in R/Python. The framework sum_coded scores are relative indicators only (higher = better health). See RAND SF-36 scoring manual for official 0–100 transformation.
citation: Hays, R. D., Sherbourne, C. D., & Mazel, R. M. (1993). The RAND 36-Item Health Survey 1.0. Health Economics, 2(3), 217–227. https://doi.org/10.1002/hec.4730020305
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: MOS Patient Adherence Scale
description: 5-item general measure of patient adherence to medical recommendations from the Medical Outcomes Study. Items 1–4 use a 6-point frequency scale (1=None of the time to 6=All of the time) with question head 'How often was each of the following statements true for you during the past 4 weeks?' Item 5 asks overall adherence frequency. Items 1 and 3 are reverse-coded. Score is mean of 5 items after reversing items 1 and 3; higher scores indicate greater adherence. Internal consistency alpha = 0.81. Public Domain (RAND Corporation).
citation: Hays, R. D. (n.d.). The Medical Outcomes Study (MOS) Measures of Patient Adherence. RAND Corporation. https://www.rand.org/health-care/surveys_tools/mos/patient-adherence.html
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: MOS Sexual Problems Measures
description: Brief measures of sexual problems from the Medical Outcomes Study (MOS). Consists of separate item sets for men (4 items) and women (5 items) assessing the frequency of sexual problems during the past 4 weeks. Men's items cover: lack of interest in sex, inability to relax and enjoy sex, difficulty achieving or maintaining an erection, and difficulty with ejaculation. Women's items cover: lack of interest in sex, inability to relax and enjoy sex, difficulty becoming sexually aroused, difficulty with lubrication, and difficulty achieving orgasm. Scoring: mean of applicable items (1–6 scale); transform to 0–100 via ((mean − 1) / 5) × 100; lower scores indicate fewer sexual problems. Items are administered based on respondent sex; branching logic may be needed in computerized administration. NOTE: Exact item text requires the official RAND instrument — see URL above.
citation: Sherbourne, C.D. (1992). Social functioning: Sexual problems measures. In A.L. Stewart & J.E. Ware (Eds.), Measuring functioning and well-being: The Medical Outcomes Study approach (pp. 194–204). Durham, NC: Duke University Press.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: MOS Sleep Scale
description: 12-item scale measuring sleep quality and quantity from the Medical Outcomes Study. Item 1 is sleep onset latency (multi, 5 options). Item 2 is sleep quantity (short, hours per night). Items 3–12 are 6-point frequency items (1=All of the time to 6=None of the time). Past 4 weeks recall. Public Domain (RAND Corporation).
citation: Hays, R. D., & Stewart, A. L. (1992). Sleep measures. In A. L. Stewart & J. E. Ware Jr. (Eds.), Measuring Functioning and Well-Being (pp. 235–259). Duke University Press.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: MOS Social Support Survey
description: 20-item multidimensional measure of perceived social support developed for the Medical Outcomes Study. Item 1 is a structural measure (number of close friends and close relatives). Items 2–20 rate the perceived availability of four types of functional support on a 5-point frequency scale (1=None of the time to 5=All of the time). Subscale scores are transformed to a 0–100 metric: Score = ((mean_of_items − 1) / 4) × 100. Higher scores indicate greater perceived social support.
citation: Sherbourne, C.D., & Stewart, A.L. (1991). The MOS Social Support Survey. Social Science & Medicine, 32(6), 705–714. https://doi.org/10.1016/0277-9536(91)90150-B
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Multidimensional Academic Procrastination Scale
description: 15-item self-report measure of academic procrastination with three dimensions: Core Procrastination (6 items), Poor Time Management (4 items), and Work Disconnection (5 items). Items 8, 9, 12, and 13 are reverse-scored. 5-point scale (1-5).
citation: Gonzalez-Brignardello, M. P., & Sanchez-Elvira Paniagua, A. (2023). Dimensional structure of MAPS-15: Validation of the multidimensional academic procrastination scale. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 20(4), 3201. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph20043201
license_explanation: Published in IJERPH (MDPI) under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Multidimensional Workplace Compassion Scale
description: 12-item self-report measure of other-oriented/expressed compassion in the workplace. Assesses four components of workplace compassion: noticing (recognizing distress), empathizing (feeling distress), sensemaking (understanding context), and acting (responding to distress).
citation: Mandliya, A., & Pandey, J. (2023). The development and validation of multidimensional workplace compassion scale: Linking its conceptualization and measurement. Frontiers in Psychology, 14, 1142309. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2023.1142309
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychology under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: National Eye Institute Visual Function Questionnaire-25
description: 25-item self-report measure of vision-related quality of life developed by the National Eye Institute and RAND Corporation. Assesses 12 subscales covering general health, general vision, ocular pain, near and distance activities, social functioning, mental health, role difficulties, dependency, driving, color vision, and peripheral vision. All subscales are scored 0–100 (higher = better visual function). Subscale score = mean of recoded item values × 25; see Mangione et al. (2001) for full recoding tables. A composite score (mean of all subscales excluding general health) is also commonly reported.
citation: Mangione, C.M., Lee, P.P., Gutierrez, P.R., Spritzer, K., Berry, S., & Hays, R.D. (2001). Development of the 25-item National Eye Institute Visual Function Questionnaire. Archives of Ophthalmology, 119(7), 1050–1058. https://doi.org/10.1001/archopht.119.7.1050
license_explanation: RAND Corporation and NEI place their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Negative Perceptions of Science Scale
description: 20-item scale measuring negative perceptions of science across four dimensions: Corrupt (5 items), Onerous (5 items), Heretical (5 items), and Limited (5 items). 5-point Likert scale. Final validated version from Table 4 of Morgan, Wisneski, & Skitka (2018).
citation: Morgan, G. S., Wisneski, D. C., & Skitka, L. J. (2018). Identifying relevant anti-science perceptions to improve science-based communication: The Negative Perceptions of Science Scale. Social Sciences, 7(4), 64. https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci7040064
license_explanation: Published in MDPI Social Sciences under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: OpenScales Feature Demonstration
description: Comprehensive demonstration scale exercising all major OSD specification features: consent gate (S8); all question types (likert, multi, multicheck, short, long, vas, grid, inst); sections with randomization (S4); section-order randomization; parameter-driven branches (A1); visible_when on items and sections including is_answered operator (S1); input validation (C9); per-item likert override; likert_reverse display; randomize_options on multi; per-item question_head override; VAS with named anchors and vertical orientation; forward/reverse scoring with norms and score transforms; computed variables (boolean and numeric); answer_alias and answer piping (S3); text substitution via parameters (S2); selectable dimensions (C7). Use this scale to verify all engine features work correctly after changes.
citation: OpenScales internal demonstration scale — not for research use.
license_explanation: Public domain demonstration scale. Not for research use.

name: Oxford Agoraphobic Avoidance Scale
description: 8-item measure of agoraphobic avoidance and distress. For each of 8 everyday activities, respondents indicate (a) whether they could do it now (0) or would be too anxious (1), yielding an avoidance score of 0–8, and (b) how anxious they would feel doing it on a 0–10 scale (0=No distress, 10=Extreme distress), yielding a distress score of 0–80. Higher scores indicate greater agoraphobic avoidance and distress.
citation: Lambe, S., Bird, J. C., Loe, B. S., Rosebrock, L., Kabir, T., Petit, A., Mulhall, S., Jenner, L., Aynsworth, C., Murphy, E., Jones, J., Powling, R., Chapman, K., Dudley, R., Morrison, A., O'Regan, E., Yu, L.-M., Clark, D. M., Waite, F., & Freeman, D. (2021). The Oxford Agoraphobic Avoidance Scale. Psychological Medicine. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291721002713

name: Oxford Positive Self Scale
description: 24-item measure of positive self-beliefs associated with psychological well-being. Participants rate how much they believe each statement about themselves over the past week. Scores range from 0 to 96; higher scores indicate stronger positive self-beliefs.
citation: Freeman, D., Rosebrock, L., Loe, B. S., Saidel, S., Freeman, J., & Waite, F. (2023). The Oxford Positive Self Scale: psychometric development of an assessment of cognitions associated with psychological well-being. Psychological Medicine, 53(15), 7161–7169. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291723000624

name: Paranormal and Supernatural Beliefs Scale
description: 13-item measure of paranormal and supernatural beliefs. 4-point Likert scale (0=Strongly Disagree to 3=Strongly Agree). Items 7, 8, and 12 are reverse scored. Total score is the sum of coded items.
citation: Dean, C. E., Akhtar, S., Gale, T. M., Irvine, K., Wiseman, R., & Laws, K. R. (2021). Development of the Paranormal and Supernatural Beliefs Scale. Frontiers in Psychology. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t93032-000]

name: Patient Health Questionnaire-9
description: 9-item depression screening tool measuring symptom severity over the past 2 weeks. Scores range from 0 to 27. A score of ≥10 has 88% sensitivity and 88% specificity for major depression. Note: The PHQ-8 is this scale with item 9 (suicidal/self-harm thoughts) omitted, for use in contexts where adequate intervention for suicidal ideation cannot be provided (e.g., large population surveys). PHQ-8 uses the same scoring cutoffs. See Kroenke et al. (2009), https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jad.2008.06.026.
citation: Kroenke, K., Spitzer, R. L., & Williams, J. B. (2001). The PHQ-9: Validity of a brief depression severity measure. Journal of General Internal Medicine, 16(9), 606-613. https://doi.org/10.1046/j.1525-1497.2001.016009606.x
