name: Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire - Short Form
description: 18-item short form of the Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (PSQ-III) measuring patient satisfaction with medical care across 7 subscales. Items use a 5-point Strongly Agree to Strongly Disagree scale. Favorably worded items (+) are reverse-coded so that higher subscale scores indicate greater satisfaction. Subscale scores are means of constituent items (range 1–5). Public Domain (RAND Corporation).
citation: Marshall, G.N., & Hays, R.D. (1994). The Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire Short Form (PSQ-18). RAND, Santa Monica, CA. P-7865.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire III
description: 51-item measure of patient satisfaction with medical care across 7 subscales: General Satisfaction (6 items), Technical Quality (10 items), Interpersonal Manner (7 items), Communication (5 items), Financial Aspects (8 items), Time Spent with Doctor (2 items), and Access/Availability/Convenience (12 items). Item 30 is not scored. Items use a 5-point Strongly Agree to Strongly Disagree scale. Favorably worded items (+) are reverse-coded so that higher subscale scores indicate greater satisfaction. Subscale scores are sums of constituent items. Public Domain (RAND Corporation).
citation: Hays, R. D., Davies, A. R., & Ware, J. E. (1987). Scoring the Medical Outcomes Study Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire: PSQ-III. MOS Memorandum. RAND Corporation.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Pediatric Quality of Life Inventory Generic Core Scales (Child Self-Report, Ages 8–12)
description: 23-item self-report measure of health-related quality of life for children aged 8–12 years. Covers 4 dimensions: Physical Functioning (8 items), Emotional Functioning (5 items), Social Functioning (5 items), and School Functioning (5 items). Response scale: 0=Never a problem to 4=Almost always a problem. Items are reverse-scored and linearly transformed to a 0–100 scale: (4 − response) / 4 × 100. Higher scores indicate better quality of life. A Total Score is the mean of all 23 items; a Physical Health Summary Score is the Physical Functioning subscale; a Psychosocial Health Summary Score is the mean of Emotional, Social, and School Functioning items. NOTE: Parallel parent-proxy report forms exist for children ages 2–18. Disease-specific PedsQL modules (e.g., asthma, cancer, diabetes) are available separately.
citation: Varni, J.W., Seid, M., & Rode, C.A. (1999). The PedsQL: Measurement model for the Pediatric Quality of Life Inventory. Medical Care, 37(2), 126–139. https://doi.org/10.1097/00005650-199902000-00003
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Penn State Worry Questionnaire
description: 16-item measure of the trait of worry. Scores range from 16 to 80; higher scores indicate greater worry severity.
citation: Meyer, T. J., Miller, M. L., Metzger, R. L., & Borkovec, T. D. (1990). Development and validation of the Penn State Worry Questionnaire. Behaviour Research and Therapy, 28(6), 487-495. https://doi.org/10.1016/0005-7967(90)90135-6
license_explanation: Confirmed public domain by original author Thomas D. Borkovec (personal communication to Rudolf Cardinal, 2015-07-23, documented in CamCOPS). Also described as public domain at multiple sources and reproduced in full in Lam et al. (2005) Assessment Scales in Depression, Mania and Anxiety.

name: PHQ Panic Disorder Module
description: 15-item yes/no panic disorder screening module from the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ). Items 1–4 are gate questions about anxiety attacks. Items 5–15 assess 11 somatic symptoms during the last bad anxiety attack. Panic Syndrome is diagnosed if items 1–4 are all YES and 4 or more of items 5–15 are YES. Items 2–15 are only shown if item 1 is YES. Free to use (Pfizer/PHQ Screeners).
citation: Spitzer, R. L., Kroenke, K., & Williams, J. B. W. (1999). Validation and utility of a self-report version of PRIME-MD: The PHQ Primary Care Study. JAMA, 282(18), 1737–1744. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.282.18.1737
license_explanation: Pfizer released all PHQ and GAD-7 screeners without copyright restriction. No permission is required to reproduce, translate, display, or distribute.

name: Positive Functioning Inventory
description: 12-item measure of subjective well-being and positive psychological functioning. Items describe positive and negative feelings experienced in the past seven days. Scores range from 0 to 36, with higher scores indicating greater well-being. Negative items are reverse-coded for the total score.
citation: Joseph, S., & Maltby, J. (2014). Positive functioning inventory: initial validation of a 12-item self-report measure of well-being. Psychology of Well-Being, 4, 15. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13612-014-0015-6

name: Positive Relationships Scale (PRIM+19)
description: 19-item measure of positive relationships (PRIM+19). Three subscales: Development of Positive Bonds (6 items), Management of Interpersonal Relationships (5 items), Integration in the Social Circle (8 items). Scored 0 (does not describe me) to 3 (describes me very well). All items direct; higher scores indicate greater positive relationships.
citation: Vilca, L. W., Aquino-Hidalgo, J. M., Esteban-Brañes, J., & Caycho-Rodríguez, T. (2023). Development and psychometric study of the scale of the positive relationship PRIM+19 in Peruvian university students. BMC Psychology, 11(1). https://doi.org/10.1186/s40359-023-01094-6. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t95443-000]
license_explanation: Published in BMC Psychology under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Protective Behavioral Strategies for Marijuana Scale
description: 17-item short form assessing the frequency with which young adults use protective behavioral strategies when using marijuana. Response scale: 1=Never to 6=Always. Higher scores indicate more frequent use of protective strategies and are negatively associated with marijuana use and consequences. The 17-item version was developed using IRT to be free from bias on gender, race, ethnicity, and recreational marijuana legal status. Total scores can be converted to IRT-scaled T-scores (M=50, SD=10) via the scoring transform.
citation: Pedersen, E. R., Huang, W., Dvorak, R. D., Prince, M., Hummer, J. F., & Marijuana Outcomes Study Team. (2017). The Protective Behavioral Strategies for Marijuana Scale: Further examination using Item Response Theory. Psychology of Addictive Behaviors, 31, 548–559. https://doi.org/10.1037/adb0000291
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Psychosocial fActors Relevant to BrAin DISorders in Europe–24 (PARADISE 24)
description: PARADISE 24 is a measure to assess "the impact of brain disorders on people’s lives, based on psychosocial difficulties that are experienced in common across brain disorders". Intellectual property rights Auto-converted from CamCOPS; requires manual review.
citation: PARADISE Consortium (2015). PARADISE 24: A Measure to Assess the Impact of Brain Disorders on People's Lives. *PLoS One* 10: e0132410. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/26147343; article https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0132410; Supplementary File 1 https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0132410.s001; Supplementary File 2 https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0132410.s002.
license_explanation: CamCOPS IP notes: Copyright © 2015 Cieza et al. | This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are properly credited. | The original open-acc

name: PTSD Checklist for DSM-5
description: 20-item self-report measure of PTSD symptoms over the past month, aligned with DSM-5 criteria. Scores range from 0 to 80, with a provisional PTSD diagnosis suggested at a score of 31-33 or higher.
citation: Weathers, F. W., Litz, B. T., Keane, T. M., Palmieri, P. A., Marx, B. P., & Schnurr, P. P. (2013). The PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5). National Center for PTSD. Available at: https://www.ptsd.va.gov
license_explanation: Public Domain (U.S. Government work).

dimensions.name: Negative Alterations in Cognition and Mood (Criterion D)
Alterations in Arousal and Reactivity (Criterion E)

name: Quality of Life in Epilepsy Inventory-31
description: 31-item patient inventory measuring quality of life in epilepsy across 7 subscales: Seizure Worry, Overall QOL, Emotional Well-Being, Energy/Fatigue, Cognitive Functioning, Medication Effects, and Social Functioning. Mixed response formats. Items derived from the QOLIE-89. Past 4 weeks recall. Copyright 1993 RAND. Public Domain.
citation: Cramer, J. A., Perrine, K., Devinsky, O., Bryant-Comstock, L., Meador, K., & Hermann, B. (1998). Development and cross-cultural translations of a 31-item quality of life in epilepsy inventory. Epilepsia, 39(1), 81–88.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain.

name: Quality of Life in Epilepsy Inventory-89
description: 89-item self-report measure of health-related quality of life for adults with epilepsy. The QOLIE-89 is the full version of the QOLIE-31, covering 17 health concepts across four summary domains: Epilepsy-Targeted, Mental Health, Physical Health, and Cognitive. All subscales scored 0–100 (higher = better quality of life). A weighted composite overall score is computed. NOTE: Exact item text requires the official RAND instrument — see URL above. Item text provided here reflects the published structure from Devinsky et al. (1995) and is based on the MOS/SF-36 item pool plus epilepsy-specific items.
citation: Devinsky, O., Vickrey, B.G., Cramer, J., Perrine, K., Hermann, B., Meador, K., & Hays, R.D. (1995). Development of the quality of life in epilepsy inventory. Epilepsia, 36(11), 1089–1104. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1528-1157.1995.tb00467.x
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Questionnaire on Internet Search Dependence
description: 12-item self-report measure of dependence on Internet search engines. Scores range from 0 to 48, with four subscales: Habitual Use, External Storage, Withdrawal, and Internet Search Trust.
citation: Wang, Y., Wu, L., Zhou, H., Xu, J., & Dong, G. (2016). Development and validation of a self-reported questionnaire for measuring internet search dependence. Frontiers in Public Health, 4, 274. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2016.00274

name: RAADS-14 Screen
description: 14-item screening tool for autism spectrum disorder in adults, derived from the 80-item RAADS-R. Scores range from 0 to 42. A cutoff score of ≥14 is recommended for autism spectrum screening.
citation: Eriksson, J. M., Andersen, L. M. J., & Bejerot, S. (2013). RAADS-14 Screen: Validity of a screening tool for autism spectrum disorder in an adult psychiatric population. Molecular Autism, 4(1), 49. https://doi.org/10.1186/2040-2392-4-49

name: RAND Asthma Quality of Life 12-Item Short Form
description: 12-item short form of the RAND-IAQL item bank measuring asthma-specific quality of life. Items selected by IRT calibration from the 65-item bank. Items use the same stems and response scales as the full bank. Sum score range 0–48; IRT T-score conversion available from RAND.
citation: Hays, R.D., Leake, B.D., Sherbourne, C.D., Coons, S.J., Spritzer, K.L., & Bjorner, J.B. (2012). Development of a measure of asthma-specific quality of life. Quality of Life Research, 21(7), 1103–1113.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain.

name: RAND Asthma Quality of Life 4-Item Ultra-Brief Form
description: 4-item ultra-brief form of the RAND-IAQL item bank. The 4 most informative items selected by IRT calibration for rapid screening of asthma-specific quality of life. Sum score range 0–16; IRT T-score conversion available from RAND.
citation: Hays, R.D., Leake, B.D., Sherbourne, C.D., Coons, S.J., Spritzer, K.L., & Bjorner, J.B. (2012). Development of a measure of asthma-specific quality of life. Quality of Life Research, 21(7), 1103–1113.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain.

name: RAND Asthma Quality of Life Item Bank
description: 65-item bank measuring asthma-specific health-related quality of life. Developed using item response theory (IRT). Items 1–18 and 63–65 use the stem 'because of my asthma'; items 19–62 use 'In the past 4 weeks'. Items 1–62 use a 5-point impact scale; items 63–65 use a 5-point frequency scale. Response range 0–4; higher = greater negative impact. Short forms of 12 items (RAND-IAQL-12) and 4 items (RAND-IAQL-4) are available.
citation: Hays, R.D., Leake, B.D., Sherbourne, C.D., Coons, S.J., Spritzer, K.L., & Bjorner, J.B. (2012). Development of a measure of asthma-specific quality of life. Quality of Life Research, 21(7), 1103–1113.
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain.

name: Regret and Disappointment Scale
description: 7-item instrument for assessing regret and disappointment in decision making using cognitive appraisal items (without using the words 'regret' or 'disappointment'). Items distinguish self-caused negative outcomes (internal attribution, regret) from other-caused negative outcomes (external attribution, disappointment). Regret Index = mean of items 2 and 4; Disappointment Index = mean of items 3 and 5. Items rated on a 7-point scale (1 = Not at all, 7 = Totally agree). Item 7 is a forced-choice counterfactual completion.
citation: Marcatto, F., & Ferrante, D. (2008). The Regret and Disappointment Scale: An instrument for assessing regret and disappointment in decision making. Judgment and Decision Making, 3(1), 87-99. https://doi.org/10.1017/S193029750000019X

name: Regret Elements Scale
description: 10-item measure of post-decisional regret distinguishing two components: the affective element (RES-A, 5 items) capturing the emotional/feeling component of regret, and the cognitive element (RES-C, 5 items) capturing counterfactual thinking and mental simulation. Items are rated on a 7-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly Disagree, 7 = Strongly Agree). Higher subscale scores indicate stronger affective or cognitive regret.
citation: Buchanan, J., Summerville, A., Lehmann, J., & Reb, J. (2016). The Regret Elements Scale: Distinguishing the affective and cognitive components of regret. Judgment and Decision Making, 11(3), 275-286. https://doi.org/10.1017/S1930297500003107

name: Revised Green et al. Paranoid Thoughts Scale
description: 18-item measure of paranoid thinking over the past month. Comprises an 8-item Ideas of Reference subscale and a 10-item Ideas of Persecution subscale. Higher scores indicate greater paranoia.
citation: Freeman, D., Loe, B. S., Kingdon, D., Startup, H., Molodynski, A., Rosebrock, L., Brown, P., Sheaves, B., Waite, F., Bird, J. C., Carr, L., & Clark, D. M. (2021). The revised Green et al., Paranoid Thoughts Scale (R-GPTS): psychometric properties, severity ranges, and clinical cut-offs. Psychological Medicine, 51(2), 244–253. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291719003155

name: Reward and Punishment Responsivity and Motivation Questionnaire
description: 18-item questionnaire measuring reward and punishment responsivity and motivation. Four subscales: Reward Responsivity (RR, 4 items), Motivation to Approach Reward (MR, 5 items), Punishment Responsivity (PR, 5 items), Motivation to Avoid Punishment (MP, 4 items). 5-point scale (1 = does not apply to me at all; 5 = applies to me completely). Higher scores indicate greater responsivity or motivation.
citation: Jonker, N. C., Timmerman, M. E., & de Jong, P. J. (2022). Frontiers in Psychology. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2022.868963. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t95928-000]
license_explanation: Items published in Jonker et al. (2022), Frontiers in Psychology, which publishes under CC BY 4.0.

name: Ritvo Autism Asperger Diagnostic Scale-Revised
description: 80-item self-report instrument for diagnosing autism spectrum disorder in adults (18+) with average or above-average intelligence. Four subscales: Social Relatedness, Circumscribed Interests, Language, and Sensory Motor. Total score range 0–240; a score of 65 or greater is consistent with ASD. Designed for clinician-administered use.
citation: Ritvo, R. A., Ritvo, E. R., Guthrie, D., Ritvo, M. J., Hufnagel, D. H., McMahon, W., Tonge, B., Mataix-Cols, D., Jassi, A., Attwood, T., & Eloff, J. (2011). The Ritvo Autism Asperger Diagnostic Scale-Revised (RAADS-R): A scale to assist the diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder in adults: An international validation study. Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders, 41(8), 1076–1089. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-010-1133-5
license_explanation: The RAADS-R is published in open access and freely available for clinical and research use.

name: Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale
description: 10-item measure of global self-esteem. Scored 0-30 with higher scores indicating higher self-esteem.
citation: Rosenberg, M. (1965). Society and the Adolescent Self-Image. Princeton University Press.
license_explanation: The Morris Rosenberg Foundation grants permission for use of the scale for educational and professional research. See https://socy.umd.edu/about-us/using-rosenberg-self-esteem-scale

name: S-Five Misophonia Scale
description: 25-item multidimensional measure of misophonia covering appraisals, perceived threat, outbursts, and functional impact. Each item is rated on an 11-point scale (0–10).
citation: Vitoratou, S., Uglik-Marucha, N., Hayes, C., & Sandhu, G. (2021). Listening to people with misophonia: Exploring the multiple dimensions of sound intolerance using a new psychometric tool, the S-Five, in a large sample of individuals identifying with the condition. Psych, 3(4), 664–678. https://doi.org/10.3390/psych3040041

name: Satisfaction With Life Scale
description: 5-item scale measuring global cognitive judgments of one's life satisfaction.
citation: Diener, E., Emmons, R. A., Larsen, R. J., & Griffin, S. (1985). The Satisfaction with Life Scale. Journal of Personality Assessment, 49(1), 71–75. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327752jpa4901_13
license_explanation: The SWLS is in the public domain and therefore you are free to use it without permission or charge by all professionals (researchers and practitioners) as long as you give credit to the authors of the scale: Ed Diener, Robert A. Emmons, Randy J. Larsen and Sharon Griffin as noted in the 1985 article in the Journal of Personality Assessment.

name: Scientific Bullshit Receptivity Scale
description: 20-item scale measuring receptivity to scientific bullshit. Ten items are plausible-sounding but scientifically nonsensical statements (bullshit items, dimension: sbr) created by taking existing physical laws and replacing central words with randomly selected physics glossary terms. Ten items are real scientific statements based on actual physical laws (dimension: factual). Participants rate how truthful each statement is on a 5-point scale (1 = Not at all truthful; 5 = Very truthful). The primary score is the mean rating of the 10 scientific bullshit items (alpha = .83); higher scores indicate greater receptivity to scientific bullshit. Note: the factual items were included in Studies 1 and 2 only, not Study 3.
citation: Evans, A. M., Sleegers, W., & Mlakar, Z. (2020). Individual differences in receptivity to scientific bullshit. Judgment and Decision Making, 15(3), 401-412.

name: Screening Instrument for Borderline Personality Disorder
description: 5-item ultra-brief screening tool for borderline personality disorder (BPD). Each item maps to a DSM-5 BPD criterion. 4-point scale (0–3). Sum score 0–15; cutoff >9 recommended (sensitivity 56%, specificity 92%). AUC-ROC = 0.83.
citation: Jucktrakul, K., Wongpakaran, T., Wongpakaran, N., et al. (2020). Development and validation of a screening instrument for borderline personality disorder (SI-Bord) for use among university students. BMC Psychiatry, 20, 479. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12888-020-02807-6
license_explanation: Published in BMC Psychiatry under CC BY 4.0.

name: Self-Compassion Scale
description: 26-item self-report measure of self-compassion across six subscales: Self-Kindness, Self-Judgment (R), Common Humanity, Isolation (R), Mindfulness, and Over-Identification (R). 5-point Likert scale (1 = Almost never, 5 = Almost always). Total score is the mean of the six subscale means after reverse-scoring the three negative subscales. Range 1–5.
citation: Neff, K. D. (2003). Development and validation of a scale to measure self-compassion. Self and Identity, 2, 223–250. https://doi.org/10.1080/15298860309027
license_explanation: The SCS is freely available for research and educational use from https://self-compassion.org/

name: Self-Compassion Scale – Short Form
description: 12-item short form of the Self-Compassion Scale (SCS; Neff, 2003), assessing six facets of self-compassion: Self-Kindness (items 2, 6), Self-Judgment (items 11, 12; negative), Common Humanity (items 5, 10), Isolation (items 4, 8; negative), Mindfulness (items 3, 7), and Over-Identification (items 1, 9; negative). Two items per facet. Items use a 5-point scale (1=Almost Never to 5=Almost Always). Facet subscale scores are the means of the two relevant items. The total Self-Compassion score is the mean of all 12 items after reverse-scoring the three negative facets (Self-Judgment, Isolation, Over-Identification). Higher total scores indicate greater self-compassion.
citation: Raes, F., Pommier, E., Neff, K. D., & Van Gucht, D. (2011). Construction and factorial validation of a short form of the Self-Compassion Scale. Clinical Psychology & Psychotherapy, 18(3), 250–255. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291710001078

name: Self-Consciousness Type Scale
description: 14-item self-report measure of self-consciousness based on regulatory focus theory. Distinguishes between promotion-focused self-consciousness (growth-oriented) and prevention-focused self-consciousness (defensive). All items begin with the stem 'I think a lot about myself because....' Note: The original preliminary scale had 18 items; after factor analysis, 4 items were removed, yielding 14 final items.
citation: Author(s). (2024). Development and validation of the self-consciousness type scale. Frontiers in Psychology, 15, 1362324. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2024.1362324
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychology under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Short Maximization Inventory (Nenkov et al.)
description: 9-item shortened version of the Schwartz et al. (2002) Maximization Scale with three subscales: Alternative Search (3 items), Decision Difficulty (3 items), and High Standards (3 items). Developed through factor-analytic reduction of the original 13-item scale. The three subscales should be examined separately rather than summed into a total score. Items use a 7-point completely disagree to completely agree scale.
citation: Nenkov, G. Y., Morrin, M., Ward, A., Schwartz, B., & Hulland, J. (2008). A short form of the Maximization Scale: Factor structure, reliability and validity studies. Judgment and Decision Making, 3(5), 371-388.

name: Smartphone Addiction Proneness Scale for Youth
description: 15-item screening tool for smartphone addiction proneness in youth (elementary, middle, and high school students). Four subscales: Disturbance of Adaptive Functions, Virtual Life Orientation, Withdrawal, and Tolerance. 4-point Likert scale (1–4). Three items are reverse-scored (8, 10, 13). Total score range 15–60.
citation: Kim, D., Lee, Y., Lee, J., Nam, J. K., & Chung, Y. (2014). Development of Korean Smartphone Addiction Proneness Scale for Youth. PLoS ONE, 9(5), e97920. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0097920
license_explanation: Published in PLoS ONE under Creative Commons Attribution License.

name: Smartphone Addiction Scale - Short Version
description: 10-item short version of the Smartphone Addiction Scale measuring problematic smartphone use. Scores range from 10 to 60. At-risk cutoffs: ≥31 for males, ≥33 for females.
citation: Kwon, M., Kim, D.-J., Cho, H., & Yang, S. (2013). The Smartphone Addiction Scale: Development and validation of a short version for adolescents. PLOS ONE, 8(12), e83558. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0083558

name: Smartphone Distraction Scale
description: 16-item self-report scale measuring smartphone distraction across four dimensions: Attention Impulsiveness (distraction from notifications and the device itself), Online Vigilance (cognitive preoccupation with checking content and social validation), Multitasking (simultaneous smartphone use during other activities), and Emotion Regulation (using the phone to manage negative affect and avoid unpleasant tasks). Higher scores indicate greater smartphone distraction.
citation: Throuvala, M. A., Pontes, H. M., Tsaousis, I., Griffiths, M. D., Rennoldson, M., & Dhuffar, M. K. (2021). Exploring the dimensions of smartphone distraction: Development, validation, measurement invariance, and latent mean differences of the Smartphone Distraction Scale (SDS). Frontiers in Psychiatry, 12, 642634.

name: Social and Economic Conservatism Scale
description: 12-item measure of social and economic conservatism using feeling thermometer ratings (0-100). Higher composite scores indicate greater conservatism.
citation: Everett, J. A. C. (2013). The 12 item Social and Economic Conservatism Scale (SECS). PLoS ONE, 8(12), e82131. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0082131

name: Social Media Use Disorder Scale for Adolescents
description: 10-item ICD-11-based self-report scale assessing social media use disorder in adolescents aged 10–17 years, adapted from the Gaming Disorder Scale for Adolescents (GADIS-A). Two factors: Cognitive Behavioral Symptoms (items 1,2,4,5; cutoff >8) and Negative Consequences (items 3,6,7,8,9; cutoff >6). Item 10 is a time criterion. Disorder is indicated when both factor cutoffs are reached and the time criterion is met.
citation: Paschke, K., Austermann, M. I., Simon-Kutscher, K., & Thomasius, R. (2021). ICD-11-Based Assessment of Social Media Use Disorder in Adolescents: Development and Validation of the Social Media Use Disorder Scale for Adolescents. Frontiers in Psychiatry, 12, 661483.

name: Social Value Orientation Slider Measure
description: 15-item measure of social value orientation assessing the degree to which individuals value outcomes for others relative to themselves. Six primary items yield an SVO angle classifying respondents as altruistic (>57.15°), prosocial (22.45° to 57.15°), individualistic (-12.04° to 22.45°), or competitive (<-12.04°). Nine optional secondary items further distinguish inequality aversion from joint gain maximization among prosocial respondents.
citation: Murphy, R. O., Ackermann, K. A., & Handgraaf, M. J. J. (2011). Measuring Social Value Orientation. Judgment and Decision Making, 6(8), 771-781.

name: Socially Desirable Response Set Survey
description: A 5-item scale measuring the tendency to give socially desirable responses. Used as a methodological covariate to identify response bias in self-report surveys.
citation: Hays, R. D., Hayashi, T., & Stewart, A. L. (1989). A five-item measure of socially desirable response set. Educational and Psychological Measurement, 49(3), 629–636. https://doi.org/10.1177/001316448904900315
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. See https://www.rand.org/health/surveys/sdrs.html

name: Sociotype Questionnaire
description: 16-item measure of social relationships across four domains. Four subscales: Family (items 1-4), Friends (items 5-8), Acquaintances (items 9-12), Peers/Colleagues (items 13-16). Scored 0 (never) to 5 (always); items 10 and 12 are reverse scored. Higher scores indicate stronger social connections.
citation: Marijuan, P. C., Montero-Marin, J., Navarro, J., Garcia-Campayo, J., & del Moral, R. (2017). The 'sociotype' construct. PLOS ONE. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0185551. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t67623-000]
license_explanation: Items published in Marijuan et al. (2017), PLOS ONE, which publishes under CC BY 4.0.

name: Spirituality of Science Scale
description: 10-item measure of the extent to which individuals experience science as a source of spirituality. 7-point Likert scale. Single total score computed as the mean of all items.
citation: Preston, J. L., Coleman III, T. J., & Shin, F. (2025). Frontiers in Psychology. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t94796-000]

name: Stress and Anxiety to Viral Epidemics-6
description: 6-item measure assessing anxiety response to viral epidemic threats, covering fear of contagion, health worry, and avoidance concerns. Scores range from 0 to 24.
citation: Chung, S., Kim, H. J., Ahn, M. H., Yeo, S., Kim, K., Jeong, J., Lee, J., & Suh, S. (2021). Development of the Stress and Anxiety to Viral Epidemics-6 items (SAVE-6) scale for assessing the anxiety response of the general population to the viral epidemic during the COVID-19 pandemic. Frontiers in Psychology, 12, 669606. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2021.669606

name: Stress-Related Growth Measure
description: 5-item measure of stress-related growth. 5-point Likert scale (1=Not suitable for me to 5=Very suitable for me). Single total score computed as the sum of all items.
citation: Yildirim, M., & Arslan, G. (2021). Frontiers in Psychology. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t86073-000]

name: System Usability Scale
description: 10-item scale measuring perceived system usability. Traditional SUS score (0-100) = 25 * (mean_coded - 1).
citation: Brooke, J. (1996). SUS: A 'Quick and Dirty' Usability Scale. In P. W. Jordan, B. Thomas, I. L. McClelland, & B. Weerdmeester (Eds.), Usability Evaluation in Industry (pp. 189-194). https://doi.org/10.1201/9781498710411

name: Traits of Creative Potential Questionnaire
description: 12-item self-report measure assessing three dimensions of creative potential: Transgression (items 1, 4, 7, 10), Challenge (items 2, 5, 8, 11), and Exploration (items 3, 6, 9, 12). 7-point scale.
citation: Penagos-Corzo, J. C., & Saucedo, A. (2021). Development and validation of a questionnaire to assess creative potential traits. Frontiers in Psychology, 12, 756079. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2021.756079
license_explanation: Published in Frontiers in Psychology under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

name: Trust in AI Scale
description: 8-item scale measuring user assessments of trust in AI systems, with reverse-coded wary item.
citation: Hoffman, R. R., Mueller, S. T., Klein, G., & Litman, J. (2023). Measures for explainable AI: Explanation goodness, user satisfaction, mental models, curiosity, trust, and human-AI performance. Frontiers in Computer Science, 5, 1096257.

name: University Student Engagement Inventory
description: 15-item multidimensional measure of student engagement in higher education across behavioural, emotional, and cognitive dimensions. Validated across seven countries.
citation: Marôco, J., Marôco, A. L., Campos, J. A. D. B., & Fredricks, J. A. (2016). University student's engagement: Development of the University Student Engagement Inventory (USEI). Psicologia: Reflexão e Crítica, 29, 21. https://doi.org/10.1186/s41155-016-0042-8

name: User Experience Questionnaire
description: 26-item semantic differential scale measuring user experience across 6 dimensions: Attractiveness, Perspicuity, Efficiency, Dependability, Stimulation, and Novelty.
citation: Laugwitz, B., Held, T., & Schrepp, M. (2008). Construction and evaluation of a user experience questionnaire. In A. Holzinger (Ed.), HCI and Usability for Education and Work, LNCS 5298 (pp. 63-76). Springer.
license_explanation: Free for all purposes.

name: User Experience Questionnaire - Short Version
description: 8-item short version of the User Experience Questionnaire measuring pragmatic and hedonic quality of interactive products using semantic differential items on a 7-point scale (-3 to +3).
citation: Schrepp, M., Hinderks, A., & Thomaschewski, J. (2017). Design and Evaluation of a Short Version of the User Experience Questionnaire (UEQ-S). International Journal of Interactive Multimedia and Artificial Intelligence, 4(6), 103-108. https://doi.org/10.9781/ijimai.2017.09.001

name: Visit-Specific Satisfaction Instrument
description: 9-item measure of patient satisfaction with a specific outpatient visit, covering access to care (items 1–4), interaction with the provider (items 5–8), and overall visit satisfaction (item 9). Scores are transformed to a 0–100 scale; higher scores indicate greater satisfaction.
citation: Rubin, H. R., Gandek, B., Rogers, W. H., Kosinski, M., McHorney, C. A., & Ware, J. E. (1993). Patients' ratings of outpatient visits in different practice settings: Results from the Medical Outcomes Study. JAMA, 270(7), 835–840. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1993.03510070069036
license_explanation: RAND Corporation places their health survey instruments in the public domain. RAND explicitly states these instruments are available for use without permission or fee.

name: Words Can Harm Scale
description: 10-item scale measuring the belief that words can cause lasting psychological harm. Items assess attitudes about speech in general and about reading words specifically. Responses on a 1–100 sliding scale (1 = Strongly disagree, 25 = Somewhat disagree, 50 = Neither agree nor disagree, 75 = Somewhat agree, 100 = Strongly agree). Total score is the mean of all 10 items.
citation: Lovett, B. J., & Bhatt, R. (2025). Words Can Harm Scale (WCHS). PsyArXiv. https://osf.io/gf8j5/overview

name: Work-Nonwork Balance Crafting Scale
description: 16-item scale measuring proactive strategies for crafting work-nonwork balance. Two subscales: WNBC-work (8 items) assessing crafting balance from the work side, and WNBC-nonwork (8 items) assessing crafting balance from the nonwork side. 5-point Likert scale (1-5).
citation: Kerksieck, P., Brauchli, R., de Bloom, J., Shimazu, A., Kujanpaa, M., Lanz, M., & Bauer, G. F. (2022). Frontiers in Psychology. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2022.892490. [APA PsycTests: https://doi.org/10.1037/t95741-000]
license_explanation: Items published in Kerksieck et al. (2022), Frontiers in Psychology, which publishes under CC BY 4.0.
